title: Distance Not Safety
persona:
## 4Castle

The Future of Rock, The Power of Harmony

**4Castle **is a genre-defying, five-piece supergroup under Maricopa Records, built on a foundation of powerful vocal harmonies, intricate musicianship, and fearless innovation. Rooted in rock, but unbound by limitations, their music fuses elements of progressive rock, funk, Latin rhythms, blues, and folk, delivering a sound that is both technically complex and emotionally resonant.

### **A Band Like No Other**

Each member of **4Castle **brings a distinct voice-both musically and vocally-to the mix:
🎸 **Raven Cortez**(Lead Guitar, Vocals) – A fiery virtuoso with Latin and blues influences, weaving face-melting solos into every performance.
🎸 **Skylar “Sky” Monroe**(Rhythm Guitar, Vocals) – The poetic soul of the band, crafting melodic foundations and lyrically rich storytelling.
🎹 **Jonah Vega**(Keyboards, Vocals) – The sonic architect, layering psychedelic synths, Hammond organ swells, and cinematic orchestrations.
🎸 **Malcolm “Mack” Bishop**(Bass, Vocals) – The groove master, blending funk, jazz, and progressive rock into fluid, deep-pocket basslines.
🥁 **Eli “Fox” Foster **(Drums, Vocals) – The wild heartbeat, pushing the band forward with thunderous fills, polyrhythmic grooves, and explosive energy.

### **What Defines 4Castle?**

- **Multi-Layered Vocal Harmonies**– All five members **sing**, creating **rich, choral-like textures**that elevate every song.

- **Complex Arrangements & Bold Songwriting**– Each track is an **immersive journey**, balancing **technical mastery with raw emotion**.

- **Rhythmic Power & Innovation**– From **tight funk grooves to shifting time signatures**, their music **moves you-literally and figuratively**.

- **Lyrically Challenging Yet Accessible**– Their songs explore **deep themes of life, change, and resilience**, wrapped in **memorable hooks and melodies**.

### **4Castle’s Sound**

Imagine putting Emerson, Lake & Palmer, Heart, Led Zeppelin, Sly Stone, and Carlos Santana in a blender. The result? **4Castle**. Their music pays homage to the greats while pushing rock’s boundaries into the future.

### **Join the Journey**

With Maricopa Records, **4Castle **is set to redefine what a modern rock band can be. Expect genre-bending albums, electrifying performances, and music that resonates across generations.

🚀 Follow **4Castle **as they build their legacy-one song, one harmony, and one unforgettable moment at a time.

[](https://open.spotify.com/artist/4Cmxf7xuTHzf7UahMK2xJh)[](https://music.apple.com/us/artist/4castle/1801052673)![](https://www.maricoparecords.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/5-rooks-in-a-dark-setting-1.png)
## Releases

review.review_text: Distance Not Safety opens with 'Cut Flowers' in waltz time, a tender, minor-key meditation that tells you exactly what kind of band 4Castle wants to be: technically assured, emotionally direct, unafraid of a central metaphor. From there the album builds a convincing arc, climbing through the propulsive title track and the country-rock confidence of 'Double Black Diamonds' before 'Belligerent' and 'Venus Syndrome' push the tempo and the stakes to their breaking point. 'Getting Through' is the one moment where the album catches its breath a little too long, but a single measured step back in a ten-track run is easy to forgive. 'Void of Intention' closes things with shimmering resolve rather than a grand gesture, which turns out to be the right call: after the intensity of the back half, restraint reads as earned rather than deflating.

track_reviews_in_context.review_text: The album's strongest moment doubles as its opening statement, and that's a bold call that pays off completely. 'Cut Flowers' sets the terms: 3/4 time, genuine emotional weight, and a central metaphor that the band earns rather than just announces. The image of something severed from its roots and pressed between pages gives the chorus real stakes, and the dynamic discipline here, pulling back to near-silence in the verses before the harmonies stack into something close to a freight train on the refrain, establishes immediately that 4Castle are playing a long game. 'You gave me petals, not the roots' lands with the kind of plainspoken sting that reminds me of what John Hiatt does best, saying something complicated in one clean line. Opening an album here is a statement of intent: this is a band confident enough to lead with a waltz-time ballad and trust that it will hold the room. It does.

Where 'Cut Flowers' pulled back and let the silence do the work, 'Distance Not Safety' arrives at a propulsive clip and makes clear the album can shift gears without losing its footing. The chorus hook, 'you can run from the fire, but you still breathe the flame,' lands exactly as hard as it should, and the thematic line is clean: the opener was about what gets taken from you, this one is about what you carry yourself. The bridge, 'it's not the miles, it's the weight you carry,' earns its breath before the full band closes things out. Two tracks in, the album has already established both its emotional range and its craft ceiling, and this one confirms neither the opener nor the sequence is a fluke.

After two tracks running at or above anthemic, 'Getting Through' provides the album its first genuine exhale without stopping moving. The 'I'm good. I'm getting through' hook lands harder in sequence because by now the listener trusts that 4Castle don't throw lines away; when the song earns it with four minutes of patient, specific grief, that payoff means something. The pulse keeps pushing even when the narrator can't, and the guitar solo around the three-minute mark stays articulate rather than showy, which is the right call here. This is a dependable cut doing structural work: it carries the album forward without demanding to be the center of attention, and the one hook that stays with you longer than you expect is exactly what mid-sequence needs.

'Getting Through' kept the album moving through grief; 'The Dreamer and the Dreamed' is where 4Castle let the tempo settle and the harmonies do the heavy lifting. At a steady pace in a bright major key, the track breathes in a way that a harder push would ruin, and arriving here at track four, that spaciousness feels earned rather than indulgent. The contrast between the male and female vocal registers gives the chorus, 'I'm the dreamer and the dreamed, caught inside this waking stream,' real lift, like early Fleetwood Mac finding the same note at the same time, and the filtered breakdown two-thirds through resets the energy cleanly before the final section lands. The Castaneda closing line, 'For me there is only walking on paths that have heart,' earns its mysticism rather than just wearing it. One of the more fully realized things they've done with this kind of slow-burn material, and in this sequence it marks the album's midpoint turn: from carrying weight to asking what you're walking toward.

After the open-sky contemplation of 'The Dreamer and the Dreamed,' 'Double Black Diamonds' locks back into a confident country-rock groove and doesn't let go. The tempo lift feels natural rather than forced, and the female lead cresting over the full band on that refrain delivers payoff that the album has been building toward since the opener's dynamic restraint. 'Cold on my face, but my temperature's rising' earns its keep as a hook, nodding toward the anthemic clarity of early Heart without losing 4Castle's own fingerprints. At the album's midpoint, this is exactly the right kind of wide-open, windows-down track: it doesn't demand anything from the listener, it just delivers.

The back half of the album opens with its most ambitious piece, and 'Cat's Claw' earns that positioning. Built around a Southwestern legend of a shaman's bargain gone wrong, it threads an ancient story into something universal with 'hopeful, we make the bargain, tho the ending is set,' and the band's five-part harmony is exactly the right vehicle for that fatalism. The verses settle into a spare, intimate folk-rock feel before the chorus opens up at a brisk clip into something close to classic rock anthemic, the kind of lift that calls to mind early Heart at their most declarative. Coming off 'Double Black Diamonds' and heading toward the harder tracks ahead, 'Cat's Claw' serves as the album's mythological center of gravity: the moment where the stakes feel oldest and the harmonies feel biggest.

Whatever comfort 'Cat's Claw' offered in communal harmony, 'Belligerent' burns it down at a punishing pace. The verse simmers with a coiled, clean-guitar tension before the chorus detonates into full-band fury, and that dynamic whiplash is the whole point. In sequence, this is the album's most confrontational moment, and it hits harder because the preceding tracks have built enough trust to make the outburst feel earned rather than cheap. The lyric 'who the hell am I yelling for' does the song's real work, turning a rant about outrage culture back on the ranter with enough self-awareness to keep it from being preachy. At just over three minutes, 4Castle don't overstay their welcome, and 'raise the stakes, raise the choir' is built for a crowd to throw back at the stage. The sing-along is the release valve the album needed at track seven.

'Belligerent' asked who you're yelling for; 'Delusion of Truth' answers by widening the lens to the whole room. At a driving tempo, it hits the ground running and doesn't let up, with the rhythm section locked in tight and the chorus, 'Welcome to the bubble, the Delusion of Truth,' landing with the kind of communal punch that Audioslave used to own. The pre-chorus, 'I'll bet you won't let go / Of the shit you think you know,' is blunt in the best way, and the layered vocals make it feel like a full room agreeing with you. In sequence, these two tracks form a hard-rock diptych: one turning the rage inward, the next pointing outward. The production is dense and loud without turning to mud, which is harder to pull off than it sounds, and it sustains the album's energy heading into the final two.

After two consecutive hard-rock gut-punches, 'Venus Syndrome' arrives at an even faster pace and keeps the pressure up rather than releasing it, which is the right call: the album doesn't lose momentum, it sharpens it. The opening guitar riff lands like a wall, and 4Castle never let the pressure drop across its tight 3:33. 'Thought she was Venus, shining bright' is the kind of chorus that earns its shout, and the bridge line 'being hot is no virtue' gives the cautionary tale enough wit to keep it from feeling like a grudge track. Punchy and direct, with layered guitars that push hard without burying the lead vocal, 'Venus Syndrome' is the album's penultimate peak: it burns everything down so that 'Void of Intention' can begin in the ash and build back toward something worth the journey.

Everything the album has built, the grief, the drive, the mythology, the rage, arrives here to be resolved without being explained. 'Void of Intention' earns its title by doing the opposite: those shimmering, reverb-soaked guitars build with real purpose across twelve sections, and the lyric 'breath, the traveller, guides an empty mind' lands with the kind of quiet conviction that makes a four-minute-forty track feel exactly the right length. At a brisk pace that mirrors 'Distance Not Safety' without replicating it, the closer completes a circle: where the album's second track insisted you carry the weight, this one suggests you might be allowed to set it down. The guitar solo that crowns the back half is the kind of melodic, soaring statement that reminds you of the best moments on a Heart or early Santana record, less flash than feeling. A worthy close to a collection that earns more as a whole than any single piece promises on its own.
